questions.question: Explain why the fork() system call can be problematic in a multi-threaded program.
keyPoints: The fork() system call duplicates the entire process, including all of its threads.
However, in some implementations, fork() only duplicates the thread that called it, leading to a potential incomplete or inconsistent process state in the child.
This ambiguity can cause issues like deadlocks if the forked thread held locks that other (now missing) threads were waiting on.

questions.question: Describe the concept of thread cancellation and the two main approaches to implementing it.
keyPoints: Thread cancellation is the task of terminating a thread before it has completed its work.
Asynchronous cancellation terminates the target thread immediately, which is risky as it might leave shared data in an inconsistent state or not free allocated resources.
Deferred cancellation allows the target thread to periodically check if it should terminate, providing a safer point for cleanup and resource release.

questions.question: Compare and contrast the three common thread libraries: POSIX Pthreads, Windows threads, and Java threads. Discuss their primary implementation models and key characteristics.
keyPoints: POSIX Pthreads is a standard API (specification) typically implemented at the user level on many UNIX/Linux systems, though it can also be supported by kernel-level threads; it is highly portable across systems.
Windows threads are kernel-level threads implemented in the Windows OS; they are tightly integrated with the kernel, providing good concurrency and leveraging multiprocessors effectively.

Java threads are implemented using a native thread library (like Pthreads or Windows threads) by the JVM; the model (many-to-one, many-to-many) depends on the specific JVM implementation, but they offer a high-level, platform-independent interface for multithreading.
